title: Inverse Melting of 3D Antiferromagnetic Order in Multi-sublattice Magnetic Perovskites
authors: Bangye Qin, Dmitry D. Khalyavin, Ran Liu, Kazunari Yamaura, Alexei A. Belik, Roger D. Johnson
abstract: In conventional antiferromagnets a long-range ordered 3D ground state transitions to a disordered paramagnetic state on warming, often via lower dimensional spin correlations within the critical regime. Here we demonstrate a striking departure from this paradigm. Through analysis of neutron powder diffraction data, we show that the magnetic ground state of columnar-ordered quadruple perovskites, Na$R$Mn$_2$Ti$_4$O$_{12}$ ($R$ = Dy, Sm), lacks long-range order, hosting only 2D spin correlations. On warming, this disordered state transitions into a 3D long-range ordered antiferromagnetic structure prior to the phase transition to the paramagnetic state. Our results establish an unconventional order-by-heating mechanism in which intrinsic A-site chemical disorder is coupled to competing exchange interactions between the rare earth and Mn sub-lattices, leading to a novel type of magnetic phase transition.
summary: The study reports on a novel 'order-by-heating' phenomenon in perovskite antiferromagnets where the material enters a disordered state at low temperatures. Using neutron diffraction, the authors characterize this disorder as stacking faults whose nature is controlled by the rare earth element. This establishes a new mechanism for magnetic phase transitions driven by coupling between chemical disorder and competing exchange interactions.
main_problem: The study investigates an unconventional magnetic transition where long-range 3D antiferromagnetic order appears upon heating from a disordered ground state, termed 'inverse melting'.
main_result: Both $	ext{NaDyMn}_2	ext{Ti}_4	ext{O}_{12}$ and $	ext{NaSmMn}_2	ext{Ti}_4	ext{O}_{12}$ exhibit this inverse melting, with the specific nature of the disorder (Ising-like faults vs. spin canting) dictated by the rare earth ion's anisotropy.
method: The research relies heavily on Neutron Powder Diffraction (NPD) measurements across a temperature range and Rietveld refinement analysis to characterize magnetic correlations.
model_or_system: The physical system is columnar-ordered quadruple perovskites, $	ext{NaRMn}_2	ext{Ti}_4	ext{O}_{12}$ ($	ext{R} = 	ext{Dy}, 	ext{Sm}$), which possess multiple magnetic sublattices and intrinsic A-site chemical disorder.
key_observables: Magnetic Bragg intensities, peak broadening (indicating dimensionality of order), incommensurate satellite reflections, and the temperature dependence of the Mn magnetic moment.
important_parameters: Temperature range (1.5 K to 20 K); rare earth ion type ($	ext{Dy}^{3+}$ vs $	ext{Sm}^{3+}$) which controls anisotropy; stacking fault probability ($p$).
main_assumptions: The disorder can be modeled using magnetic stacking faults, and the transition mechanism is governed by competing exchange interactions coupled to A-site disorder.
figures_summary: Figures show NPD patterns at various temperatures for both compounds, illustrating peak changes below $T^*$. One figure compares experimental $\{100\}$ peaks with calculated patterns derived from fault models, demonstrating temperature dependence of the fault probability.
paper_structure: The paper analyzes NPD data to characterize the magnetic ground state (disordered) and tracks its evolution upon warming into a 3D ordered phase. The analysis distinguishes between different types of disorder based on rare earth chemistry and uses computational modeling to fit observed peak changes.
why_interesting: This work provides an experimental realization of complex, non-standard phase transitions in magnetic insulators, linking structural disorder (A-site vacancies) directly to the emergence/disappearance of long-range magnetic order via thermal cycling.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. (a,d) The crystal structure of NaRMn2Ti4O12. The split MnO4 square-planar units (Mn1 coordination) are shown in purple, with the ideal Mn1 positions indicated by grey spheres. The MnO4 tetrahedra (Mn2 coordination) are shown in blue, and the TiO6 octahedra in light yellow. Oxygen anions are shown in light blue, Ti ions in red, and (Na/R) cations as black spheres. Crystallographic axes are indicated for reference. (b,e) Isolated views of the Mn1 and Mn2 poly- hedra, illustrating the alternating columnar ordering of the Mn sublattice. (c,f) The long-range ordered C-type magnetic structure, where arrows indicate the orientation of the ordered magnetic moments, and circles with dots and...

FIG. 2. (a) Neutron powder diffraction (NPD) patterns of NaDyMn2Ti4O12 measured at T = 1.5, 6, and 12 K. (b) Ri- etveld refinement of the NPD data at T = 6 K, showing the experimental data (black dots), calculated profile (red line), and difference curve (blue line). The vertical tick marks in- dicate the Bragg reflection positions of the nuclear phase of the main perovskite structure (top row), the TiO2 impurity phase (middle row), and the magnetic phase (bottom row). (c) Neutron powder diffraction pattern at T = 1.5 K. The right insets in panels (a)–(c) show an enlarged view of the d-spacing region around the {100} magnetic reflections.

FIG. 3. (a) Neutron powder diffraction (NPD) patterns of NaSmMn2Ti4O12 measured at T = 1.5, 7, and 14 K. (b) Ri- etveld refinement of the NPD data at T = 7 K, showing the experimental data (black dots), calculated profile (red line), and difference curve (blue line). The vertical tick marks indi- cate the Bragg reflection positions of the nuclear phase of the main perovskite structure (top row), the TiO2 impurity phase (middle row), and the magnetic phase (bottom row). (c) Neu- tron powder diffraction pattern at T = 1.5 K. The first three rows of vertical tick marks correspond to the same nuclear, impurity, and commensurate magnetic phases as in panel (b), while the fourth row indicates...

FIG. 5. Comparison between experimental and calculated scattering patterns for the {100} magnetic Bragg peak of NaRMn2Ti4O12 (R = Dy, Sm). (a-d) The {100} magnetic Bragg peak from NaDyMn2Ti4O12 measured on cooling. (e,f) The {100} magnetic Bragg peak from NaSmMn2Ti4O12 at 7 K and 1.5 K. The experimental neutron powder diffraction data are shown as black dots, and the calculated scattering patterns (see text for details) are shown as orange curves. The magnetic stacking-fault probability, p, is indicated in each panel.

FIG. 4. Temperature dependence of the Mn magnetic moment in NaRMn2Ti4O12. (a) NaDyMn2Ti4O12, in which Ising-like commensurate Mn spin order (blue squares) and Mn spin disorder (orange circles) are observed. (b) NaSmMn2Ti4O12, in which orthogonal commensurate (blue circles) and pseudo-incommensurate (oranghe triangles) Mn spin components are observed. The dashed lines represent fits of a phenomenological power-law expression to the total moment.
